metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the generated email includes all required key facts accurately and naturally.

rubric.description: The email ignores the required facts or contradicts them.
Only a tiny fragment of one required fact appears.
Some facts are present, but multiple important facts are missing or distorted.
Most facts are present, but one important fact is missing, weakened, or ambiguous.
Almost all required facts are present, with only a minor omission or awkward compression.
Every required fact is included accurately and naturally, with no unsupported factual claims.

metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the generated email matches the requested tone in context.

rubric.description: The tone is absent, opposite of the requested tone, or unusable for the context.
The tone is very far from the requested tone.
The tone only loosely resembles the requested tone and is noticeably mismatched.
The tone is partly aligned but generic, uneven, or inconsistent.
The tone is mostly aligned with only small mismatches.
The tone closely and consistently matches the requested tone throughout.

metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the output works as a professional email.

rubric.description: The output is not usable as an email.
It barely resembles a professional email or collapses greeting, body, closing, and signature into a flat single paragraph.
Some email elements are present, but structure is incomplete, hard to scan, or mostly unformatted.
Basic email content is present, but paragraphing, greeting, body, or sign-off formatting is noticeably weak.
The email is clear and professional with only minor structure or formatting rough edges.
The subject and body are polished, clear, concise, and formatted with distinct greeting, body paragraphing, and sign-off.

metrics.reason: The email includes all required facts accurately: it is about the May 17 meeting at 11:00 A.M., names Kenneth Lay, Marty Sunde, and Bob Williams, and asks for exact waiting instructions about where they should wait or go upon arrival. The sender is identified as Rosalee Fleming, Kenneth Lay's assistant, and the tone is concise, polite, and professional.

The email is concise, polite, and professional throughout. It clearly asks for the exact waiting instructions, uses courteous phrasing (“Could you please,” “Thank you for your help”), and stays focused on the meeting-arrival details without unnecessary elaboration.

The email includes a clear subject line, a proper greeting (“Hi Sarah,”), a concise body that states the request clearly, and a distinct closing/sign-off (“Thank you for your help. / Best, / Rosalee Fleming”). It is well formatted and professional with the required email structure present.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it says the meeting is next week, lists the three exact available time slots, asks the Project Team to reply with the option that works best so availability can be indicated, and states the schedule should be finalized soon. It also correctly identifies Alexis Tolmie as Project Manager at Nexus Corp.

The email is professional, concise, and collaborative throughout. It clearly asks the Project Team to indicate availability for the proposed time slots and states the need to finalize the schedule soon, matching the requested tone closely and consistently.

The email includes a clear subject, a professional greeting, a well-structured body with bulleted time options, a direct request for availability, and a distinct sign-off with sender details. It is polished, concise, and easy to scan.

The email includes every required fact accurately: it references Invoice 48217, states it was due on April 30, 2026, notes the outstanding amount is $3,450, and asks for an update on the expected payment date. It also correctly identifies Nina Brooks, the Accounts Payable Team, and her role at Harborview Labs.

The email is firm, polite, and professional throughout: it clearly follows up on overdue invoice 48217, states the due date and outstanding amount, and respectfully asks for the expected payment date. The closing and signature also maintain a professional tone.

The email includes a clear subject line, a distinct greeting, a concise body with the invoice details and payment-date request, a polite closing, and a full signature. The structure is polished, easy to scan, and professionally formatted.

All required facts are included accurately: the original interview was Tuesday at 2:00 PM, there is an interviewer conflict, alternatives of Wednesday 9:00 AM and Thursday 3:00 PM are offered, the role is Software Engineer at BrightPath Software Solutions, the interview is expected to last about 90 minutes, and the candidate is asked to confirm which time works best.

The email is consistently professional, courteous, apologetic, and concise throughout. It politely explains the conflict, apologizes for the inconvenience, offers clear alternative times, and asks for confirmation without unnecessary detail.

The email is well-structured and polished: it includes a clear subject, a professional greeting, a concise body with the reason for rescheduling, the original interview time, the expected duration, two alternative time slots, and a direct request for confirmation. It also ends with an appropriate sign-off and signature.

The email includes the early-bird deadline of September 10, 2025, the conference dates of October 22 to 24, 2025, the $300 early-bird fee versus the $400 regular fee, and a clear encouragement to register soon to secure the lower rate. The only missing key fact is the current date of September 5, 2025, so the coverage is almost complete.

The email is informative and professional, with a clear reminder, exact dates, and pricing details. It also has a mildly upbeat, encouraging tone through phrases like "A quick reminder," "you can secure the early-bird rate," and "We encourage you to complete your registration soon," though it remains fairly straightforward rather than strongly upbeat.

The email includes a clear subject line, a distinct greeting, two well-separated body paragraphs, and a proper sign-off. The wording is concise and professional, with the conference dates and registration deadline clearly stated, and the affiliation included at the end.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it states the design review is expected to finish by Friday, 19 June, 2026, explains the delay was caused by missing stakeholder feedback, and says no change is needed to the final launch date yet. No required fact is missing or contradicted.

The email is transparent about the delay and its cause, calmly gives the updated completion date, reassures the recipient that the final launch date is unchanged, and maintains a steady, professional tone throughout.

The email includes a clear subject line, a professional greeting, distinct body content with paragraphing, and a proper sign-off. It is concise, well structured, and easy to scan, matching the highest rubric level.

All required facts are included accurately: the email states the meeting was about Q3 roadmap priorities, says the attached notes summarize the key decisions and action items, and asks for corrections by end of day Thursday. It also matches the sender and recipient context without adding unsupported claims.

The email is clear, organized, and professional throughout. It directly states the meeting minutes are attached, summarizes the key decisions and action items, and politely requests corrections by end of day Thursday, matching the requested tone closely and consistently.
The email includes a clear subject line, a distinct greeting, separate body paragraphs, and a proper sign-off with signature. It is well formatted and professional, matching the highest rubric level.

All required facts are included accurately: the cleanup is on Saturday from 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM, volunteers are asked to bring gloves and closed-toe shoes, and lunch and water will be provided. The sender, recipient, and affiliation are also correctly stated.

The email is consistently friendly, encouraging, and appreciative. Phrases like “We’d love your help,” “please bring gloves and closed-toe shoes,” and “we truly appreciate your support” match the requested tone well throughout, while clearly inviting volunteers to the cleanup event.

The email includes a clear subject line, a distinct greeting, well-separated body paragraphs, and a proper sign-off with the sender and affiliation. It is polished, concise, and professionally formatted.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it states the visitor policy is updated effective Monday, says visitors must check in at the front desk, and requires a government-issued photo ID for entry. The tone is formal and informative, and there are no unsupported factual claims.

The email is consistently formal and informative, matching the requested tone throughout. It clearly announces the policy update, states the front desk check-in and government-issued photo ID requirements, and notes the Monday effective date in a professional manner.

The email includes a clear subject, a formal greeting, distinct body paragraphs presenting the policy update and details, and a professional sign-off. The structure is polished, concise, and easy to scan.

The email includes all required facts accurately: maintenance is scheduled on Sunday from 1:00 AM to 3:00 AM, the portal may be unavailable during that window, and service is expected to resume immediately after maintenance. It is also brief, reassuring, and professional.

The email is brief, professional, and reassuring throughout. It clearly and calmly states the maintenance window, notes the portal may be unavailable, and reassures that service is expected to resume immediately after maintenance, which closely matches the requested tone.

The email includes a clear subject line, a distinct greeting, separate body paragraphs, and a professional sign-off. It is well formatted, brief, and easy to scan, matching the rubric for a polished professional email.
